note_data.title: Investment appraisal
keyPoints: Investment appraisal is covered in section 2.4 of Cost & Management Accounting
Manufacturing profit is the difference between transfer price and production cost of completed goods
Transfer price equals production cost of completed goods plus a percentage mark-up
Unrealised profit is profit which is not recognised until certain conditions are met
Factory profit is a percentage added to factory cost of production to arrive at transfer price
Cost of production equals prime cost plus factory overheads

definitions.definition: The production cost of completed goods plus a percentage mark-up
The difference between the transfer price and the production cost of completed goods
Profit which is not recognised until certain conditions are met
Percentage added to the factory cost of production to arrive at the transfer price

howToAnswer.instruction: Show all workings clearly. Use discount tables. State final answer with units (years for payback, % for IRR/ARR, $ for NPV).
Compare all methods calculated. State advantages and disadvantages. Give recommendation with justification.
Present arguments for and against. Consider qualitative factors. Reach a balanced conclusion.

modelAnswer.question: A company is considering investing $50,000 in new machinery. Expected cash flows are $15,000 per year for 5 years. Cost of capital is 10%. Calculate the NPV and advise whether to accept the project.
modelAnswer.answer:
Annual cash flow = $15,000
Annuity factor for 5 years at 10% = 3.791
Present value of inflows = $15,000 × 3.791 = $56,865
Initial investment = $50,000
NPV = $56,865 - $50,000 = $6,865
Recommendation: Accept the project because NPV is positive, indicating the project will increase shareholder wealth by $6,865.

commonMistakes: Confusing transfer price with actual selling price
Not understanding the difference between realised and unrealised profit
Mixing up factory profit with manufacturing profit concepts
examinerTips: Remember that manufacturing profit is calculated as transfer price minus production cost
Understand that unrealised profit has specific recognition criteria
formulas: NPV = Present Value of Inflows - Initial Investment
Payback Period = Initial Investment ÷ Annual Cash Flow (if equal)
ARR = Average Annual Profit ÷ Average Investment × 100
IRR Interpolation = L + [(NPV at L ÷ (NPV at L - NPV at H)) × (H - L)]
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Cash flow timeline showing initial outflow at Year 0 and subsequent annual inflows, with discount factors applied to each year's cash flow to calculate present values
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they asked students to calculate payback period and NPV for a new machine purchase and recommend which investment option to choose
Featured in O/N 2022 P2 Q5 (6 marks) — candidates had to explain the limitations of payback period method and suggest why a business might still use it despite these limitations
Appeared in M/J 2022 P2 Q3 (10 marks) — students needed to calculate ARR and payback period for two projects, then evaluate which method would be most appropriate for a small manufacturing business
Tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q4 (7 marks) — question focused on calculating NPV using a 10% discount rate and explaining why NPV is considered more reliable than other appraisal methods

workedExample.question: ABC Ltd is considering purchasing new machinery costing $80,000. The machine will generate the following net cash flows: Year 1: $25,000, Year 2: $30,000, Year 3: $35,000, Year 4: $20,000. Calculate the payback period and comment on whether this investment should be accepted if the company's target payback period is 2.5 years. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Set up cumulative cash flow table showing initial investment as -$80,000
Step 2: Add Year 1 cash flow: -$80,000 + $25,000 = -$55,000 (still negative)
Step 3: Add Year 2 cash flow: -$55,000 + $30,000 = -$25,000 (still negative)
Step 4: Add Year 3 cash flow: -$25,000 + $35,000 = +$10,000 (positive, so payback occurs in Year 3)
Step 5: Calculate exact payback: 2 years + ($25,000/$35,000) = 2 years + 0.71 = 2.71 years
Step 6: Compare to target and make recommendation with justification
workedExample.answer: Payback period = 2.71 years. The investment should be rejected because the payback period of 2.71 years exceeds the company's target of 2.5 years, meaning it takes too long to recover the initial investment and increases business risk.
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for correct cumulative cash flow calculation, 2 marks for accurate payback period calculation, 2 marks for clear recommendation linked to comparison with target payback period

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates calculated payback period incorrectly by simply dividing total investment by average annual cash flow' — always use cumulative cash flow method and interpolate for the exact payback point

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently confused cash flow with profit when performing investment appraisal calculations' — ensure you use net cash flows, not accounting profits, for all NPV and payback calculations

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates often failed to provide balanced evaluation of appraisal methods' — when comparing methods, always discuss both advantages and disadvantages of each method in relation to the specific business context
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to construct a clear investment appraisal comparison table showing: Column 1 - Time periods (Years 0-4), Column 2 - Cash flows for each year, Column 3 - Cumulative cash flows for payback calculation, Column 4 - Discount factors (if calculating NPV), Column 5 - Present values. Include clear totals and final calculations for payback period, NPV, and ARR with appropriate formulas shown.
note_data.quickRecap: Investment appraisal involves understanding manufacturing profit calculations and transfer pricing. Manufacturing profit represents the difference between transfer price and production cost. Unrealised profit must meet specific recognition criteria before being recorded in financial statements.
